back:
Central management of multiple AWS accounts — consolidated billing, Service Control Policies (SCPs) for guardrails (preventive controls that even the account root user can't bypass), and organizational units (OUs) for grouping accounts.

Foundational to almost every multi-account exam scenario; SCPs restrict what IAM policies can ever grant, they don't grant permissions themselves.

Self-service portal for downloading AWS compliance reports (SOC, PCI, ISO, etc.) and accepting agreements (like a BAA for HIPAA workloads) — purely a compliance-documentation repository, not a security control itself.

Continuously collects evidence of resource configurations/activity against prebuilt or custom compliance frameworks (e.g. PCI DSS, HIPAA, GDPR) to simplify audit preparation — automates the evidence-gathering that Config/CloudTrail data would otherwise require manually assembling.

Provisions, manages, and auto-renews public and private SSL/TLS certificates for use with ELB, CloudFront, and API Gateway — ACM-issued public certificates are free but can only be used with these integrated AWS services, not exported for use on arbitrary servers (that requires ACM Private CA or importing your own cert).

Dedicated, single-tenant, FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware security module giving you exclusive control over cryptographic keys — unlike KMS, AWS has zero access to your key material.

Exam trigger: strict compliance/regulatory requirements mandating a dedicated HSM or direct control over key storage hardware, where KMS's shared/managed model isn't sufficient.
User authentication/authorization for web/mobile apps, via User Pools and Identity Pools.
Automatically collects and correlates log data (VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings) into a visual, interactive graph to speed up investigating the root cause of a security finding.

Managed Microsoft Active Directory (or a lighter-weight Simple AD / AD Connector to proxy an existing on-prem AD) for applications and workloads needing native AD/LDAP integration — commonly needed for lift-and-shift Windows workloads or FSx for Windows File Server authentication.

Centrally manages AWS WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, Security Groups, and Network Firewall policies across all accounts/resources in an AWS Organization from one place — ensures new resources automatically inherit required security policies.

Exam trigger: 'enforce consistent WAF/Shield/security group rules across a multi-account organization.'

ML/threat-intelligence-based continuous threat detection analyzing CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and (with extensions) EKS audit logs and S3 data events, to flag things like compromised credentials, crypto-mining, or reconnaissance activity — a detective control that requires no agents to deploy.

Centralized SSO across multiple AWS accounts (via Organizations) and SAML-enabled third-party apps, using permission sets rather than per-account IAM users — the current recommended way to manage human access to multiple accounts, superseding the older standalone 'AWS SSO' branding.
Controls who (users, groups, roles) can do what (permissions via policies) on which resources within a single account.

Automated vulnerability scanning for EC2 instances, container images (in ECR), and Lambda functions — continuously scans for known CVEs and unintended network exposure, replacing the need for manual/periodic vulnerability assessment scripts.

Managed creation and control of cryptographic keys (envelope encryption under the hood) used to encrypt data across nearly all AWS services (S3, EBS, RDS, etc.), with detailed key-usage auditing via CloudTrail.

ML-powered service that scans S3 buckets to automatically discover, classify, and alert on sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data) — exam trigger: 'discover where sensitive data lives across many S3 buckets' without manually writing detection rules.

Managed, stateful network-layer firewall for a VPC, supporting fine-grained rules (including intrusion detection/prevention, domain filtering) beyond what Security Groups/NACLs can express — used when a scenario needs deep packet inspection or centralized traffic filtering across many VPCs (often deployed via Transit Gateway/Gateway Load Balancer for centralized inspection architectures).

Shares AWS resources (subnets, Transit Gateways, License Manager configurations, Route 53 Resolver rules, etc.) across accounts within an Organization without duplicating the resource — exam trigger: 'share a subnet/Transit Gateway attachment/etc. across multiple accounts' without creating separate copies of the resource in each account.
Stores, encrypts, and — critically — automatically rotates secrets like database credentials on a schedule, with native RDS/Aurora rotation integration.

Aggregates and normalizes security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party tools into one dashboard, and runs automated compliance checks against standards (CIS, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices) — the central 'single pane of glass' for security posture across accounts, distinct from any single detection service.

Issues short-lived, temporary security credentials — underpins IAM role assumption (AssumeRole), cross-account access, identity federation, and Cognito Identity Pools.

Every 'temporary credentials' or 'cross-account role assumption' exam scenario is ultimately STS under the hood.
DDoS protection: Shield Standard is automatic and free for all customers, covering network/transport layer (L3/L4) attacks.
Application-layer (L7) web firewall with customizable rules (SQL injection, XSS, rate-based rules, geo-blocking) attached to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, or AppSync.

front: Cognito User Pools vs. Identity Pools — what's the difference?
back:
User Pools = authentication (your own user directory with sign-up/sign-in, MFA, social/SAML/OIDC federation, issuing JWTs — answers 'who is this user').

Identity Pools = authorization to AWS resources (federate identities, including from a User Pool, to temporary, scoped AWS credentials).

front: Detective vs. GuardDuty — what's the difference?
back:
GuardDuty detects the threat.

Detective helps you investigate and analyze the 'why and how' after a GuardDuty finding surfaces.

front: IAM roles vs. users — which is correct for granting service/cross-account access?
back:
Roles — they're assumed temporarily and are the correct way to grant permissions to services or cross-account access.

Never use long-lived access keys for this. (Also remember: in policy evaluation, an explicit deny always wins over any allow.)

front: KMS vs. CloudHSM — what's the difference?
back:
KMS keys live in a shared, multi-tenant HSM fleet managed by AWS — simpler, integrates broadly.

CloudHSM gives you a dedicated, single-tenant HSM with exclusive control over the key material.

front: Secrets Manager vs. Systems Manager Parameter Store — what's the difference?
back:
Secrets Manager provides built-in automatic rotation and is priced per secret.

Parameter Store (SecureString tier) also encrypts values via KMS but has no built-in rotation and is generally free/cheaper — 'needs automatic rotation' is the signal to pick Secrets Manager.

front: What does Shield Advanced add over Shield Standard?
back: Layer 7 protection, near-real-time attack visibility, cost protection against scaling charges incurred during an attack, and access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT) — the exam trigger for Advanced is needing cost protection guarantees or 24/7 specialized DDoS support.

front: WAF vs. Shield vs. Network Firewall — what layer does each protect?
back:
WAF operates on HTTP request content at Layer 7.

Shield handles volumetric Layer 3/4 DDoS attacks.

Network Firewall operates broadly at the VPC network layer.

front: SCPs vs. IAM permissions — what's the difference?
back:
SCPs (used with AWS Organizations OUs) set the maximum permissions available to every principal in an account, including the root user — an SCP never grants anything itself; you still need an actual IAM policy to perform any action.

IAM policies operate at the principal level (users, groups, roles) and grant the real permissions.

SCP takes precedence: if a Principal is allowed an action via IAM policy but an attached SCP denies it, the SCP wins — even an Administrator IAM policy can't override an SCP-level Deny.

front: How are SCPs attached, and how does permission inheritance work?
back:
SCPs attach to the organization root, an OU, or an individual account, and each attachment either whitelists (Allow) or blacklists (Deny) AWS services/actions.

Access to any service not explicitly allowed by the SCPs on an account, its parent OUs, and the management account is denied.

An account only has the permissions permitted by every parent above it — a block at any level, implicit (left out of an Allow statement) or explicit (a Deny statement), can't be bypassed by an IAM policy in the account below, even one granting Administrator permissions.

front: Does an SCP restrict the AWS Organizations management account?
back: No — SCPs affect only principals managed by accounts that are members of the organization; the management account itself is not restricted by SCPs applied within that organization.

front: What are the two types of IAM policies?
back:
Identity-based policies, attached to an IAM user, group, or role, and resource-based policies, attached directly to an AWS resource such as an S3 bucket or KMS key.

Used together with SCPs, IAM policies give the fine-grained principal- and resource-level control that SCPs, as organization-wide guardrails, aren't meant to provide.

See the individual cards for the details and key differences of each type.

Attached to an IAM identity — a user, group, or role — and define what that identity is allowed or denied to do.

There are three flavors: AWS managed policies (created and maintained by AWS, reusable across accounts, and can't be edited by you), customer managed policies (created and maintained by you, reusable across multiple identities in your own account, and versioned so you can roll back), and inline policies (embedded directly in a single user, group, or role in a strict one-to-one relationship — deleted automatically if the identity is deleted, useful when a permission must never accidentally be reused elsewhere).

An identity-based policy never includes a Principal element, since the identity it's attached to is implicitly the principal.

Attached directly to a resource rather than to an identity — examples include an S3 bucket policy, an SNS topic policy, an SQS queue policy, a KMS key policy, and a Lambda function resource policy.

Unlike an identity-based policy, a resource-based policy must include a Principal element, specifying which account, user, role, or service the permissions apply to.

A role's trust policy is technically a resource-based policy too: it lives on the role itself and specifies who is allowed to assume it.

front: Identity-based vs. resource-based IAM policies: how does cross-account access differ?
back:
A resource-based policy can grant access directly to a principal in another AWS account — you just name the external account, user, or role as the Principal — with no need for that account to assume a role at all.

Identity-based policies alone can never grant cross-account access this way: reaching across accounts through identity-based policies requires the external principal to call STS AssumeRole on a role that has a matching identity-based policy attached, plus a trust policy (itself a resource-based policy, living on the role) explicitly allowing that principal to assume it.

front: Can an IAM policy restrict the AWS account root user, or attach to an OU?
back:
No to both — an IAM policy can only be attached to IAM users, groups, or roles, and can never restrict the root identity of the AWS account.

IAM policies also cannot be attached to OUs; only SCPs can.

Restricting the root user or applying a rule at the OU level requires an SCP, not an IAM policy.

front: IAM policy evaluation: explicit allow vs. implicit deny vs. explicit deny?
back:
Everything is an implicit deny by default.

An explicit Allow in a policy overrides that implicit deny.

But an explicit Deny always overrides an explicit Allow — no matter how many policies grant an action, a single explicit Deny anywhere in the evaluation blocks it.
